title: recommendedEffort returns effort=7 for unknown hardware concurrency (hwc=0), but hwc=0 means unknown not zero-core
description: When hwConcurrency is not passed and navigator.hardwareConcurrency is unavailable, hwc defaults to 0 (line 122). The guard `hwc > 0 && hwc <= 2` is then false, so the function returns 7 (highest effort). A device with unknown concurrency is likely low-end or headless, yet it receives the heaviest encode effort. The conservative choice for unknown hwc should be the lower value (6), consistent with how recommendedQualitySearch treats unknown hwc (falls into the slow-device fast-path via `hwc > 0 && hwc <= 2` being false — but that returns 'full' which is the aggressive path, making the two functions inconsistent with each other for the unknown-hwc case).
code_excerpt:
const hwc = hwConcurrency ?? (typeof navigator !== "undefined" ? navigator.hardwareConcurrency ?? 0 : 0);
  return hwc > 0 && hwc <= 2 ? 6 : 7; // MT tier on a 2-core device: don't pay effort-7 (CAP-7)
evidence: Line 122-123 of index.ts confirms hwc=0 when hardwareConcurrency is unavailable, and the condition `hwc > 0 && hwc <= 2` evaluates false for hwc=0, returning effort=7. An unknown-concurrency environment (e.g., constrained browser, older mobile browser that hides hardwareConcurrency) gets the heaviest effort with no conservative fallback. The comment says 'MT tier on a 2-core device: don't pay effort-7' but silently does pay it for unknown-concurrency devices.

title: recommendedQualitySearch returns 'full' for MT tiers with unknown hardware concurrency (hwc=0), inconsistent with effort heuristic intent
description: When hwc is 0 (unknown), `hwc > 0 && hwc <= 2` is false, so an MT-tier device with unknown concurrency gets 'full' quality search. This contradicts the conservative intent: an unknown-concurrency device should not be assumed high-performance. The effort function treats unknown hwc as high-end (effort=7); the quality function also treats it as high-end ('full'). The two are internally consistent but both assume unknown=high-end, which is wrong for environments where hardwareConcurrency is unavailable (often constrained environments or older browsers).
evidence: Line 130-132 confirms hwc=0 for unknown concurrency. For an MT-tier (relaxed-simd-mt or simd-mt) device with hwc=0, neither the simd branch nor the 2-core guard fires, so 'full' is returned unconditionally. This is the same conservative-default inversion as logic-1 — both heuristics treat unavailable concurrency data as high-end. Both findings share root cause (0 as fallback for unknown).

title: isSimdProbe and isRelaxedProbe fingerprints could collide if probe bytes are reordered in future
description: isSimdProbe checks only `view.includes(0xfd) && view.includes(0x0f)`. isRelaxedProbe checks `view.includes(0xfd) && view.includes(0x80) && view.includes(0x02)`. The two share the `0xfd` byte. The stubs are checked in order (thread → relaxed → simd) which prevents misclassification today because relaxed probe does not contain `0x0f`. However if PROBE_RELAXED_BYTES were ever updated to include an opcode with `0x0f` in its encoding, the simd check order being last would still be safe, but if PROBE_SIMD_BYTES ever gained `0x80` and `0x02`, it would be misidentified as relaxed. The detection is fragile byte-scanning rather than comparing the exact magic sequences from PROBE_*_BYTES constants.
evidence: The probe byte arrays are compile-time constants in the production source (PROBE_SIMD_BYTES, PROBE_RELAXED_BYTES) and are never modified at runtime. The test fingerprints correctly identify each probe today: PROBE_SIMD_BYTES contains `0xfd 0x0f` (SIMD i32x4.splat opcode) and PROBE_RELAXED_BYTES contains `0xfd 0x80 0x02` (relaxed madd opcode) — no overlap today. The finding is purely hypothetical future-breakage with no current failure scenario; the fix (compare against constants) would be a test quality improvement but is not a real bug.

title: installProbeStubs stub never returns true for wasmExceptions probe, silently leaving wasmExceptions=false in all matrix tests
description: The PROBE_EH_BYTES exception-handling probe does not match any of isThreadProbe, isRelaxedProbe, or isSimdProbe (it contains no 0xfd, 0xfe, 0x80, 0x02, or 0x0f bytes in the body). The stub's final `return false` always fires for the EH probe. Since none of the matrix tests assert on caps.wasmExceptions, the wasmExceptions capability is untested across all tier combinations. A wasmExceptions=true case is never exercised.
evidence: PROBE_EH_BYTES at index.ts lines 52-58 contains `0x06, 0x40, 0x19, 0x0b, 0x0b` — no `0xfd` or `0xfe` byte in the function body. isThreadProbe checks for `0xfe`, isRelaxedProbe and isSimdProbe check for `0xfd`, so the EH probe always hits the `return false` fallback. No matrix test asserts `caps.wasmExceptions === true`, meaning a browser that supports EH but the stub incorrectly reports false would be undetected. This is a real test coverage gap for the wasmExceptions capability.

title: beforeEach does not delete stale SharedArrayBuffer stub when originalSharedArrayBuffer was undefined
description: afterEach correctly deletes the SAB stub when originalSharedArrayBuffer is undefined (line 100-101: `else { delete (globalAny as any).SharedArrayBuffer; }`). But beforeEach (line 83-85) only restores SAB if it was originally present — it never deletes a stub left by a prior test. If a test calls setSAB(true) and then throws before afterEach can clean up, the next test's beforeEach will leave SAB in the stubbed state. The Node test runner normally runs afterEach even on failure, so in practice this is benign, but the asymmetry is a latent isolation defect.
evidence: Lines 83-85 of tier-matrix.test.ts confirm beforeEach only restores SAB when it was originally present; there is no `else { delete ... }` branch. Lines 94-103 show afterEach correctly has the delete branch. The asymmetry means a test that throws before afterEach runs (or a test runner that skips afterEach on process abort) would leave SAB stubbed. While node:test runs afterEach even on failure making this benign in current practice, the isolation asymmetry is real and inconsistent.

title: detectTier has no assertion that its output is consistent with computeCapabilities selectedWasmBuild
description: detectTier() and computeCapabilities() both derive the tier independently using identical logic, but there is no runtime or test-time assertion that `caps.selectedWasmBuild === detectTier()` for all probe combinations. A future change to one path without updating the other would go undetected. Adding a single deepEqual assertion in the matrix test harness after each freshCapsAndTier() call would catch divergence immediately.
evidence: freshCapsAndTier() at lines 68-76 of tier-matrix.test.ts calls detectTier() and getCapabilities() but never asserts caps.selectedWasmBuild === tier. The matrix tests do check selectedWasmBuild individually, but the invariant across all cases is implicit not explicit. This is a genuine test coverage opportunity that would catch divergence between detectTier() and the selectedWasmBuild path in computeCapabilities().

category: no property-based testing on math/state-heavy code
title: canUseThreadedWasm is only tested with three of four boolean combinations
description: The test at tier.test.ts:11-16 exercises (true,true), (true,false), (false,true), (false,false). All four combinations are actually covered — this is a non-issue. However, canUseThreadedWasm(false, false) is tested in tier.test.ts but not exercised in the matrix test canUseThreadedWasm test (tier-matrix.test.ts:176-179) which only checks three combinations. The missing (false,false) case in the matrix test is a minor coverage gap.
evidence: tier.test.ts line 15 explicitly tests `canUseThreadedWasm(false, false)` and asserts false. The (false,false) case IS covered — just in a different test file. The gap in tier-matrix.test.ts is redundant coverage, not a real test gap for this predicate. The finding self-acknowledges 'all four combinations are actually covered'.

title: WebAssembly.validate probes run twice on first getCapabilities() call due to computeCapabilities calling probes then detectTier
description: computeCapabilities() calls _probeSimd() at line 222, _probeRelaxedSimd() at line 224, and then calls detectTier() at line 256. Because _cachedTier is not yet set when computeCapabilities runs, detectTier() re-runs all the same WebAssembly.validate probes from scratch (lines 95-107 of detectTier). On the relaxed-simd-mt path this means _probeSimd() and _probeRelaxedSimd() are each called twice per first getCapabilities() invocation. WebAssembly.validate is synchronous and fast, but these are unnecessary duplicate calls. Fix: call detectTier() first in computeCapabilities and derive wasmSimd/wasmRelaxedSimd from the already-cached tier result, or cache individual probe results at module level.
evidence: Code at lines 220-226 confirms _probeSimd(), _probeWasmThreads(), _probeRelaxedSimd(), _probeWasmExceptions() are called directly. Then line 256 calls detectTier(), which when _cachedTier is undefined (first call) re-runs _probeSimd() at line 95 and _probeRelaxedSimd() at line 107 within detectTier(). A caller that invokes getCapabilities() without a prior detectTier() call triggers all probes twice. The common path freshCapsAndTier() in tests calls detectTier() first which caches the tier, but production callers using only getCapabilities() would double-probe.

title: Individual probe results (_probeSimd, _probeRelaxedSimd, etc.) are not cached at module level
description: _probeSimd(), _probeRelaxedSimd(), _probeWasmThreads(), and _probeWasmExceptions() each call WebAssembly.validate on every invocation with no result memoization. The only memoization is at the detectTier() and getCapabilities() level. If _resetCache() is called (e.g. in tests), or if detectTier() and computeCapabilities() are called in an order that causes double probing (see performance-1), the underlying validate calls repeat unnecessarily. Caching the boolean result of each probe at module level (similar to how _cachedTier is stored) would eliminate all duplicate validate calls and make _resetCache behavior explicit.
evidence: Lines 60-82 confirm the probe functions call WebAssembly.validate on every invocation with no per-probe caching. The performance-1 finding shows a concrete scenario where probes run twice. This is a real opportunity: caching probe results at module level alongside _cachedTier would eliminate redundant validate calls. WebAssembly.validate is fast but these capabilities never change within a page lifetime.

title: probeWebGpuAdapter() crashes on Node.js: bare navigator access without guard
description: Every other `navigator` access in this file is guarded with `typeof navigator !== 'undefined'`. Line 283 accesses `(navigator as any)?.gpu` directly without that guard. In Node.js (where `navigator` is undefined) this throws `ReferenceError: navigator is not defined` at runtime, making the exported public API unsafe on Node. The function is exported and callers in a Node environment could reasonably call it.
evidence: The bare `navigator` access at line 283 is inside the `try { ... } catch { return false; }` block at lines 282-286. In Node.js (pre-21 where navigator is undefined as a bare identifier), the ReferenceError thrown by `navigator` would be caught by the surrounding catch, returning false safely. The optional chain `?.gpu` is irrelevant to the ReferenceError but the catch handles it. Node.js 21+ defines navigator as a global, so no ReferenceError. This is not a crash — the catch block prevents it. However, the inconsistency with all other navigator accesses in the file is a style/readability concern worth fixing.

title: Capabilities.libjxlVersion is permanently 'unknown' despite a typed string contract
description: The `Capabilities` interface declares `libjxlVersion: string`, implying a real version is available. The implementation always assigns the literal `'unknown'` with a TODO comment. Any consumer branching on this field (e.g. `if (caps.libjxlVersion.startsWith('0.11'))`) will silently receive `'unknown'` forever. The contract should either be `libjxlVersion: string | null` with `null` meaning unavailable, or the field should be removed/typed as a discriminated union so callers cannot misuse it.
code_excerpt: libjxlVersion: "unknown", // TODO(packages/jxl-wasm/scripts/build.mjs): emit consumable libjxl version const
evidence: Line 269 of index.ts confirms `libjxlVersion: "unknown"` is hardcoded with a TODO. The interface at line 152 declares `libjxlVersion: string` with no indication the value is always 'unknown'. A consumer checking `caps.libjxlVersion !== 'unknown'` before using it would work, but nothing in the type system enforces this. Typing it as `string | null` with null meaning unavailable would make the contract honest.

title: recommendedEffort() return type declares 1–9 but only 4, 6, or 7 are reachable
description: The declared return type `1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9` implies callers should handle all nine values. In practice only 4, 6, and 7 are ever returned. This misleads consumers who write exhaustive switch statements or numeric range checks, and means the public contract is broader than the implementation. Either narrow the return type to `4 | 6 | 7` or add code paths that produce the missing values.
evidence: Lines 118-124 confirm only `return 4`, `return 6`, and `return ... ? 6 : 7` are reachable. The return type union of 1-9 is overly broad and misleads callers into thinking all values are possible. Narrowing to `4 | 6 | 7` would be a non-breaking change that improves type precision.

title: Capabilities.imageDecoder means 'class exists' not 'JXL is supported', conflating two distinct facts
description: `imageDecoder` is set via `typeof (globalThis as any).ImageDecoder !== 'undefined'` — a pure class-existence check. The `probeNativeJxl()` function separately calls `ImageDecoder.isTypeSupported('image/jxl')` to determine JXL support. The field name `imageDecoder` implies JXL-decode capability, but callers checking `caps.imageDecoder` only learn that WebCodecs ImageDecoder exists in the environment, not that JXL decoding works. This is a contract semantic mismatch. `nativeJxlDecoder` already captures the real answer; `imageDecoder` should be named `webCodecsImageDecoder` or its doc comment must clearly state it is only an API presence check.
evidence: Line 232 confirms `imageDecoder` is `typeof (globalThis as any).ImageDecoder !== 'undefined'` — a class-existence check only. The probeNativeJxl() at line 168-172 calls `ID.isTypeSupported('image/jxl')` for the real JXL capability, and its result populates `nativeJxlDecoder`. The field name `imageDecoder` without qualification would lead a caller to think it means JXL decoding is available via ImageDecoder, but it only means the class exists. This naming ambiguity is real.

category: exported API dead code / internal duplication
title: canUseThreadedWasm() is exported but never called internally; detectTier() duplicates its logic inline
description: `detectTier()` (line 105) implements the SAB+COI check inline rather than calling the exported `canUseThreadedWasm(hasSab, crossOriginIsolated)`. If a future caller monkey-patches or overrides `canUseThreadedWasm` in tests, `detectTier` is unaffected and will produce contradictory results. The contract implied by a public export is that the library uses its own API. The fix is for `detectTier` to call `canUseThreadedWasm(hasSab, crossOriginIsolated || !isBrowser)` rather than replicating the predicate.
evidence: detectTier() at line 105 uses `hasSab && (crossOriginIsolated || !isBrowser)` which intentionally extends the predicate: on Node.js (!isBrowser), MT is allowed even without COI because Node has SAB unconditionally and no COI requirement. canUseThreadedWasm(sab, coi) encodes only the browser rule (sab && coi). These are not duplicates — they are different predicates for different contexts. Using canUseThreadedWasm internally in detectTier would be incorrect for Node.js environments. The comment in the code (CAP-2) documents this intentional divergence.

title: detectTier() returns 'scalar' for both 'no WebAssembly' and 'WASM but no SIMD', undocumented in return type
description: The JSDoc comment on line 84 acknowledges the ambiguity but the `Tier` type and `detectTier()` signature give no indication that `'scalar'` is a catch-all for multiple distinct failure modes. `getCapabilities().selectedWasmBuild` returns `'none'` when WebAssembly is absent, which IS the correct differentiator, but `detectTier()` is also a public export. Callers who use only `detectTier()` cannot distinguish 'no WASM at all' from 'WASM but scalar-only' without reading internal comments. The fix is to add a JSDoc `@returns` clause or narrow the type to make the caller pattern explicit.
evidence: The JSDoc comment at lines 84-88 explicitly documents this behavior: 'Returns "scalar" both when WebAssembly lacks SIMD and when WebAssembly is entirely absent; consumers that must distinguish should use getCapabilities().selectedWasmBuild'. The limitation is already documented in the JSDoc. Callers reading the public API docs would see this note. This is a documentation finding at most, not a contract defect — the behavior is intentional and documented.

title: No runtime schema validation: Capabilities object is never structurally verified before being returned
description: All boolean flags are set from local probes in the same function so there is little risk of stale data, but `libjxlVersion` is always `'unknown'`, `deviceMemory` can be `null`, and the `selectedWasmBuild` field depends on `detectTier()` being consistent with `wasmSimd`/`wasmRelaxedSimd` flags. There is no assertion or schema check (e.g. zod/typebox/manual) that validates the returned object matches the `Capabilities` interface at runtime. Adding a single cheap structural validation before return would catch any future field omission or drift between the interface and the builder.
evidence: The Capabilities object is built in a single deterministic function (computeCapabilities) where every field is explicitly set from local variables in the same scope. TypeScript compile-time checking already enforces the return type matches the interface — if any field were omitted or mistyped, tsc would reject the code. Runtime schema validation would be redundant for a small, closed function that doesn't accept external data. Adding zod/typebox to a capabilities probe module would be an unnecessary dependency.

title: No test verifies that all fields of the Capabilities interface are present and typed correctly in the returned object
description: The tier-matrix and tier tests validate `selectedWasmBuild`, `wasm`, `wasmSimd`, and `wasmRelaxedSimd`, but fields like `webgpu`, `webnn`, `hardwareConcurrency`, `deviceMemory`, `imageDecoder`, `wasmExceptions`, `imageBitmap`, `offscreenCanvas`, and `libjxlVersion` are never asserted in any test case. If a future refactor accidentally drops or renames one of these fields, TypeScript compilation would catch it only if the type is consumed downstream — there is no runtime assertion that the full contract is fulfilled.
evidence: Reviewing both test files confirms no assertion on webgpu, webnn, hardwareConcurrency, deviceMemory, imageDecoder, wasmExceptions, imageBitmap, offscreenCanvas, or libjxlVersion. While TypeScript enforces the return type at compile time, a runtime smoke test asserting typeof on each field would catch issues (e.g., a field becoming undefined due to a probe exception that's caught). This is a genuine opportunity to catch regressions in the field set.

title: _resetCache() races with in-flight computeCapabilities() producing two simultaneous probes
description: _resetCache() sets _capsPromise = undefined synchronously. If computeCapabilities() is already in-flight (awaiting probeNativeJxl), a concurrent getCapabilities() call after reset will call computeCapabilities() again and start a second parallel probe run. Both resolve independently; callers holding the first promise get the old result while new callers get new results, but both have triggered an import() and possibly a requestAdapter(). In tests this happens every beforeEach cycle; in production it could happen if a host calls getCapabilities() then resets and calls again before resolution. The real risk is double GPU adapter requests and double @casabio/jxl-native imports.
evidence: _resetCache() at lines 6-10 sets _capsPromise synchronously to undefined, so any caller holding a reference to the old promise keeps it while new callers get a fresh computeCapabilities() run. With probeNativeJxl() containing an async await (createImageBitmap or import()), there is a real window where _resetCache() interrupts an in-flight getCapabilities() call, causing double GPU adapter requests or double native module imports. In production this is unlikely but _resetCache is exported (not underscore-private in JS), so external callers can trigger it.

category: global mutation / missing async isolation in tests
title: Test patches WebAssembly.validate on the shared global before an await without async-scoped isolation, exposing a window where concurrent test infrastructure reads the stub
description: The test at line 18 assigns to (WebAssembly as any).validate synchronously, then calls await import(...) which yields the event loop before the try/finally restores the original. Any code (e.g. node:test's own timer callbacks, the beforeEach of tier-matrix.test.ts if the two test files run in the same worker) that calls WebAssembly.validate during that await window will observe the test stub. While node:test serializes tests within a file by default, cross-file parallelism (--test-concurrency or future tooling) would cause flaky failures or silently wrong tier detection in sibling tests.
evidence: tier.test.ts lines 26-60 confirm the pattern: WebAssembly.validate is patched before `await import(...)` and restored in a finally block after the await. The await yields the event loop while the global stub is active. If node:test ever runs test files in parallel (e.g. via --test-concurrency > 1 or future tooling changes), this window exposes the stub to other test files. The tier-matrix.test.ts also patches WebAssembly.validate, so cross-file contamination is a real flakiness risk.

title: probeWebGpuAdapter() memoization via _gpuAdapterPromise is cleared by _resetCache(), allowing a second requestAdapter() call while the first is still in-flight
description: If a caller holds a reference to the promise returned by an initial probeWebGpuAdapter() call, and _resetCache() is called while gpu.requestAdapter() is awaiting, a new call to probeWebGpuAdapter() will start a second requestAdapter() call concurrently. The GPU adapter API has no documented issue with concurrent calls but the double probe wastes resources and could produce divergent results if the GPU state changes between calls. The fix is for _resetCache() to await the in-flight promise before clearing, or to document that callers must not hold references across a reset.
evidence: _gpuAdapterPromise is cleared synchronously by _resetCache() at line 9. If a caller holds the old promise while a new probeWebGpuAdapter() is called, gpu.requestAdapter() fires twice concurrently. The scenario is low-severity since _resetCache() is primarily test infrastructure, but the memoization invariant is violated across a reset. Consistent with concurrency-1.

title: probeNativeJxl() and probeWebGpuAdapter() have no timeout or AbortSignal, so a hung GPU adapter or import() hangs getCapabilities() forever
description: gpu.requestAdapter() and the dynamic import('@casabio/jxl-native') inside computeCapabilities() can theoretically hang indefinitely if the GPU driver or module resolution stalls. There is no timeout wrapper or AbortSignal. Callers of getCapabilities() could block forever with no recourse. Adding a Promise.race with a timeout (e.g. 3s) and resolving false on timeout would make the memoized capabilities safe to use in time-sensitive startup paths.
evidence: Lines 187-197 confirm createImageBitmap is awaited with no timeout. Lines 280-288 confirm gpu.requestAdapter() is awaited with no timeout. Both are memoized (_capsPromise, _gpuAdapterPromise), so a single stall permanently blocks all future callers. This overlaps with errors-2 and errors-3 which document the same issue from the error-handling angle; the finding is valid from the concurrency/AbortSignal angle as well.

title: WebAssembly.compile presence check swallows all errors silently with no diagnostic
description: The `try/catch {}` block around the `wasm` detection at line 212-214 silently swallows any unexpected exception (e.g., SecurityError, TypeError). When it fires, `wasm` stays false and every downstream capability is zeroed out, but nothing is logged. Callers have no way to distinguish 'no WASM support' from 'exception during detection'. The comment at C-9 acknowledges CSP issues but doesn't address the silent failure path.
evidence: Line 211-214 confirms the silent `catch {}`. A CSP violation or unexpected TypeError from WebAssembly.compile access would silently zero out all WASM capabilities. The comment at line 138-143 acknowledges CSP limitations but the silent failure gives no observability. While swallowing exceptions in probe code is intentional, the complete lack of a debug/diagnostic path means operators cannot diagnose why capabilities detection returns all-false.

title: createImageBitmap JXL probe has no timeout and can hang indefinitely
description: `createImageBitmap(blob)` is awaited without any timeout at line 190. On some browsers or runtime environments this call may never resolve (e.g., if the browser queues decoding tasks on a blocked compositor thread, or the MIME type is unrecognised and the implementation hangs). Because `_capsPromise` is memoised, a single hung probe permanently blocks all future `getCapabilities()` callers in that page lifetime. A `Promise.race` with a short timeout (e.g. 500 ms) would contain the blast radius.
evidence: Lines 187-197 confirm createImageBitmap is awaited with no timeout. The promise is memoized via _capsPromise at line 201-205, so a single hung createImageBitmap permanently blocks all future getCapabilities() callers. The MIME type 'image/jxl' is not universally supported, and browser implementations vary; some may queue and never resolve rather than rejecting. This is a high-severity real risk for startup reliability.

title: probeWebGpuAdapter has no timeout — gpu.requestAdapter() can stall indefinitely
description: `gpu.requestAdapter()` is awaited without a timeout at line 285. The WebGPU spec permits implementations to return a promise that resolves only after a user gesture or after driver enumeration, which can take several seconds on some systems. The memoised `_gpuAdapterPromise` means all subsequent `probeWebGpuAdapter()` callers block for the same unbounded duration. A `Promise.race` with an explicit deadline (e.g. 2 s) would protect callers.
evidence: Line 285 confirms gpu.requestAdapter() is awaited with no timeout. _gpuAdapterPromise memoizes the result, so all callers block on the same potentially-stalled promise. WebGPU driver enumeration on some systems (particularly on Linux with unstable GPU drivers) can take seconds or stall. This is the same pattern as errors-2 and is a real reliability risk for callers of probeWebGpuAdapter().

title: Dynamic import of @casabio/jxl-native swallows all errors including non-ModuleNotFound ones
description: The bare `catch {}` at line 247 swallows every failure from `import('@casabio/jxl-native')` — including transient I/O errors, SyntaxErrors in the module, and ABI mismatches — silently setting `nativeJxlDecoder = false`. A native module that fails due to a corrupted binary or version mismatch will be silently skipped, leaving the caller with no diagnostic. At minimum, non-`ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` / `MODULE_NOT_FOUND` errors should be re-thrown or at least logged.
evidence: Lines 241-248 confirm a bare `catch { /* fall through */ }` that swallows all errors from the native module import. A SyntaxError, ABI mismatch, or I/O failure would set nativeJxlDecoder=false indistinguishably from the package simply not being installed. The comment says 'fall through' but a corrupted module in a production environment would fail silently rather than surfacing an actionable error. Checking `err.code === 'ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND'` before swallowing would distinguish expected (not installed) from unexpected (broken) failures.

title: probeWebGpuAdapter accesses navigator without existence guard
description: Inside the IIFE at line 283, `(navigator as any)?.gpu` is accessed without first checking `typeof navigator !== 'undefined'`. In a non-browser Worker context or in Node.js, `navigator` is undefined at module scope and the optional-chain `?.gpu` will throw a ReferenceError before the optional chain can help, because `navigator` is not a property access but a bare identifier. The `computeCapabilities` function does guard `navigator` (line 235–238) but `probeWebGpuAdapter` is a separately exported function that can be called independently.
evidence: The bare `navigator` access at line 283 is inside a `try { ... } catch { return false; }` block. A ReferenceError thrown by undefined `navigator` in older Node.js would be caught by the surrounding catch and return false safely. In Node.js 21+, navigator is a global and no error occurs. The optional chain `?.gpu` handles the null/undefined case once navigator is resolved. The catch block provides adequate protection — the function cannot crash. The inconsistency with the guarded pattern elsewhere is a style issue, not a safety defect.

title: ImageDecoder.isTypeSupported result is not narrowed before boolean coercion — a non-boolean truthy return goes unchecked
description: `ID.isTypeSupported('image/jxl')` is awaited and used as a boolean via `if (await ...)`. The WebCodecs spec defines the return as `Promise<boolean>`, but browser implementations have historically returned other truthy values. More critically, if `isTypeSupported` returns `true` the function immediately returns `true` without verifying that `ImageDecoder` can actually instantiate — a browser could support the type string check but fail at decode time. This is a minor accuracy concern for the probe rather than a crash risk.
evidence: The WebCodecs spec explicitly defines ImageDecoder.isTypeSupported() as returning Promise<boolean>. The `if (await ...)` idiom is standard JavaScript boolean coercion that handles both boolean true and non-boolean truthy values identically. The finder's concern about 'historically truthy values' is speculative without concrete browser evidence. The probe returning true on isTypeSupported=true without full instantiation is a known probe trade-off, not an accidental oversight — full instantiation would require constructing an ImageDecoder which is more expensive.

title: No structured diagnostic channel: all probe failures are silently swallowed with no observability hook
description: All four `_probe*` functions, plus the native-module import and the `createImageBitmap` probe, use bare `catch {}` or `catch { /* fall through */ }` with no way for callers to discover what failed. In production this makes it impossible to differentiate 'SIMD disabled by browser flag' from 'WebAssembly.validate threw unexpectedly'. An optional `onDiagnostic?: (event: CapabilityDiagnostic) => void` callback on `getCapabilities()` or a debug-mode console path would let integrators surface silent failures without breaking the current API contract.
evidence: All four probe functions (lines 60-82), probeNativeJxl (lines 194-195), and the native import (lines 241-248) use silent catch blocks. A production environment where WebAssembly.validate throws unexpectedly (CSP, browser bug) would appear identical to one where SIMD is simply unsupported. An onDiagnostic callback is a well-established pattern for capability detection libraries and would genuinely improve debuggability.

title: No shared withTimeout utility — two async probes independently need timeout protection
description: `probeNativeJxl` (createImageBitmap) and `probeWebGpuAdapter` (requestAdapter) are the two async probes in this module and both currently lack timeout protection (errors-2 and errors-3). A small `withTimeout<T>(p: Promise<T>, ms: number, fallback: T): Promise<T>` utility — three lines of Promise.race — would let both probes be guarded consistently and prevent any future async probe from shipping without one.
evidence: errors-2 and errors-3 are both confirmed findings. The absence of a shared withTimeout utility is the root cause that would lead both fix sites to independently implement Promise.race. A shared utility is the correct fix pattern and would prevent the same omission in any future async probe. This is a genuine opportunity with concrete impact on the confirmed timeout findings.
